Huguenots were drawn to South Carolina by the promise of affordable land, commercial prospects, and religious freedom[5](https://columbiametro.com/article/defining-carolina/), [6](https://www.scencyclopedia.org/sce/entries/huguenots/). The Lords Proprietors actively encouraged Huguenot immigration, granting them land upon their arrival[7](https://vertexaisearch.cloud.google.com/grounding-api-redirect/AUZIYQEtymBO05EpMwHsWSwD3RGq10dvQWxFP93Rsx0WMdgtFxaOafHOJh7-jMafblSYK-lSOp2-R2ygto9oW6SqkUx0MBi58zA1zR9ydkJ0-SK4WpxnBqhGTKqt3jfGXSwdvyWNVGGTgTw_cfT-uEF_7L-3lrMHPFv7nxBKFXerHMhcD8RWbuzi), [8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf), [9](https://huguenotsociety.org/blog/passengers-on-the-margaret-1685nbsp-b5lcj), [5](https://columbiametro.com/article/defining-carolina/), [6](https://www.scencyclopedia.org/sce/entries/huguenots/). Many Huguenots purchased land directly from the proprietors in London or from their agents in Carolina[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). Examples of land transactions include: * King Charles II arranged for Huguenots to be sent to South Carolina in 1679, and some "purchased land from the Proprietors of Carolina"[7](https://vertexaisearch.cloud.google.com/grounding-api-redirect/AUZIYQEtymBO05EpMwHsWSwD3RGq10dvQWxFP93Rsx0WMdgtFxaOafHOJh7-jMafblSYK-lSOp2-R2ygto9oW6SqkUx0MBi58zA1zR9ydkJ0-SK4WpxnBqhGTKqt3jfGXSwdvyWNVGGTgTw_cfT-uEF_7L-3lrMHPFv7nxBKFXerHMhcD8RWbuzi). * In 1685, Jean Francis Gignilliat received a grant of 3,000 acres from the proprietors[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). Similarly, Arnold Bruneau was awarded a 3,000-acre land grant by the proprietors in 1686[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). * Individual Huguenots also bought land from others. For instance, Robert Stevens and Bartholomew le Roux purchased 350 acres in September 1683[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). James Le Bas bought 3,000 acres for 150 British pounds in September 1685[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). * Pierre Galliard and his wife bought land in Jamestown, a French and Swiss settlement on the Santee River[10](https://chipstone.org/article.php/289/American-Furniture-1997/Religion,-Artisanry,-and-Cultural-Identity:-The-Huguenot-Experience-in-South-Carolina,-1680%E2%80%931725). * John Gaillard acquired a total of 3,780 acres over fourteen years through both grants and purchases, including tracts from his neighbors, in the Santee community[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). * Gideon Faucheraud received a land grant in Berkeley County and also purchased land from Susan Dasseex[11](https://sites.rootsweb.com/~scbchs/goose1.html). * Jean Carrière, an indentured servant upon [5](https://www.theguardian.com/world/2004/feb/04/uselections2004.usa4), acquired land from various sources. In South Carolina, Huguenots purchased land from individuals and received grants. For example, Pierre Galliard bought land in Jamestown, a French and Swiss settlement on the Santee River[6](https://chipstone.org/article.php/289/American-Furniture-1997/Religion,-Artisanry,-and-Cultural-Identity:-The-Huguenot-Experience-in-South-Carolina,-1680%E2%80%931725). Joachim Gaillard received 600 acres in "James Town precinct" in 1687, and his son, John Gaillard, later acquired 3,780 acres through both grants and purchases[7](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). Additionally, prominent Huguenot Paul De St. Julien built Hanover House on a 1,000-acre property that was granted by the Lords Proprietors in England between 1714 and 1716[8](https://www.clemson.edu/about/history/properties/documents/hanover-activity.pdf). The Lords Proprietors encouraged Huguenot immigration to Carolina with promises of land and religious toleration, and the main areas of Huguenot settlement included the Santee River, Goose Creek, the Cooper River branches, and Charleston[6](https://chipstone.org/article.php/289/American-Furniture-1997/Religion,-Artisanry,-and-Cultural-Identity:-The-Huguenot-Experience-in-South-Carolina,-1680%E2%80%931725), [9](https://columbiametro.com/article/defining-carolina/). Individual Huguenots also settled in Virginia before and after 1700[12](https://www.newrivernotes.com/hugenot-emigration-to-virginia-and-settlement-at-manakin-town/). Some landowners and speculators, such as William Byrd I and Daniel Coxe, sought to recruit Huguenots to settle on their lands[13](http://s1030794421.onlinehome.us/religion/huguenots.html), [12](https://www.newrivernotes.com/hugenot-emigration-to-virginia-and-settlement-at-manakin-town/). There were also offers from individuals to sell large tracts of land to French immigrants, even providing financial assistance for building houses and initial sustenance[13](http://s1030794421.onlinehome.us/religion/huguenots.html). [5](https://www.theguardian.com/world/2004/feb/04/uselections2004.usa4), acquired land from various sources. In South Carolina, Huguenots purchased land from individuals and received grants. For example, Pierre Galliard bought land in Jamestown, a French and Swiss settlement on the Santee River[6](https://chipstone.org/article.php/289/American-Furniture-1997/Religion,-Artisanry,-and-Cultural-Identity:-The-Huguenot-Experience-in-South-Carolina,-1680%E2%80%931725). Joachim Gaillard received 600 acres in "James Town precinct" in 1687, and his son, John Gaillard, later acquired 3,780 acres through both grants and purchases[7](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). Additionally, prominent Huguenot Paul De St. Julien built Hanover House on a 1,000-acre property that was granted by the Lords Proprietors in England between 1714 and 1716[8](https://www.clemson.edu/about/history/properties/documents/hanover-activity.pdf). The Lords Proprietors encouraged Huguenot immigration to Carolina with promises of land and religious toleration, and the main areas of Huguenot settlement included the Santee River, Goose Creek, the Cooper River branches, and Charleston[6](https://chipstone.org/article.php/289/American-Furniture-1997/Religion,-Artisanry,-and-Cultural-Identity:-The-Huguenot-Experience-in-South-Carolina,-1680%E2%80%931725), [9](https://columbiametro.com/article/defining-carolina/). Individual Huguenots also settled in Virginia before and after 1700[12](https://www.newrivernotes.com/hugenot-emigration-to-virginia-and-settlement-at-manakin-town/). Some landowners and speculators, such as William Byrd I and Daniel Coxe, sought to recruit Huguenots to settle on their lands[13](http://s1030794421.onlinehome.us/religion/huguenots.html), [12](https://www.newrivernotes.com/hugenot-emigration-to-virginia-and-settlement-at-manakin-town/). Many Huguenots purchased land directly from the proprietors in London or from their agents in Carolina[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). Huguenots were drawn to South Carolina by the promise of affordable land, commercial prospects, and religious freedom[5](https://columbiametro.com/article/defining-carolina/), [6](https://www.scencyclopedia.org/sce/entries/huguenots/). The Lords Proprietors actively encouraged Huguenot immigration, granting them land upon their arrival[7](https://vertexaisearch.cloud.google.com/grounding-api-redirect/AUZIYQEtymBO05EpMwHsWSwD3RGq10dvQWxFP93Rsx0WMdgtFxaOafHOJh7-jMafblSYK-lSOp2-R2ygto9oW6SqkUx0MBi58zA1zR9ydkJ0-SK4WpxnBqhGTKqt3jfGXSwdvyWNVGGTgTw_cfT-uEF_7L-3lrMHPFv7nxBKFXerHMhcD8RWbuzi), [8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf), [9](https://huguenotsociety.org/blog/passengers-on-the-margaret-1685nbsp-b5lcj), [5](https://columbiametro.com/article/defining-carolina/), [6](https://www.scencyclopedia.org/sce/entries/huguenots/). Many Huguenots purchased land directly from the proprietors in London or from their agents in Carolina[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). Examples of land transactions include: * King Charles II arranged for Huguenots to be sent to South Carolina in 1679, and some "purchased land from the Proprietors of Carolina"[7](https://vertexaisearch.cloud.google.com/grounding-api-redirect/AUZIYQEtymBO05EpMwHsWSwD3RGq10dvQWxFP93Rsx0WMdgtFxaOafHOJh7-jMafblSYK-lSOp2-R2ygto9oW6SqkUx0MBi58zA1zR9ydkJ0-SK4WpxnBqhGTKqt3jfGXSwdvyWNVGGTgTw_cfT-uEF_7L-3lrMHPFv7nxBKFXerHMhcD8RWbuzi). * In 1685, Jean Francis Gignilliat received a grant of 3,000 acres from the proprietors[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). Similarly, Arnold Bruneau was awarded a 3,000-acre land grant by the proprietors in 1686[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). * Individual Huguenots also bought land from others. For instance, Robert Stevens and Bartholomew le Roux purchased 350 acres in September 1683[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). James Le Bas bought 3,000 acres for 150 British pounds in September 1685[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). * Pierre Galliard and his wife bought land in Jamestown, a French and Swiss settlement on the Santee River[10](https://chipstone.org/article.php/289/American-Furniture-1997/Religion,-Artisanry,-and-Cultural-Identity:-The-Huguenot-Experience-in-South-Carolina,-1680%E2%80%931725). * John Gaillard acquired a total of 3,780 acres over fourteen years through both grants and purchases, including tracts from his neighbors, in the Santee community[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). * Gideon Faucheraud received a land grant in Berkeley County and also purchased land from Susan Dasseex[11](https://sites.rootsweb.com/~scbchs/goose1.html). * Jean Carrière, an indentured servant upon [5](https://www.theguardian.com/world/2004/feb/04/uselections2004.usa4), acquired land from various sources. In South Carolina, Huguenots purchased land from individuals and received grants. For example, Pierre Galliard bought land in Jamestown, a French and Swiss settlement on the Santee River[6](https://chipstone.org/article.php/289/American-Furniture-1997/Religion,-Artisanry,-and-Cultural-Identity:-The-Huguenot-Experience-in-South-Carolina,-1680%E2%80%931725). Joachim Gaillard received 600 acres in "James Town precinct" in 1687, and his son, John Gaillard, later acquired 3,780 acres through both grants and purchases[7](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). Additionally, prominent Huguenot Paul De St. Julien built Hanover House on a 1,000-acre property that was granted by the Lords Proprietors in England between 1714 and 1716[8](https://www.clemson.edu/about/history/properties/documents/hanover-activity.pdf). The Lords Proprietors encouraged Huguenot immigration to Carolina with promises of land and religious toleration, and the main areas of Huguenot settlement included the Santee River, Goose Creek, the Cooper River branches, and Charleston[6](https://chipstone.org/article.php/289/American-Furniture-1997/Religion,-Artisanry,-and-Cultural-Identity:-The-Huguenot-Experience-in-South-Carolina,-1680%E2%80%931725), [9](https://columbiametro.com/article/defining-carolina/). In Virginia, another state with significant Huguenot settlement, King William sent a large group of Huguenots in 1690, allotting them lands along the James River[10](https://vertexaisearch.cloud.google.com/grounding-api-redirect/AUZIYQGLA_nm9roJkFBSFf06yDoBUVvwcAS090Uzd2zzSfmy-p9CaZjmDbauNSTC2NiYv8xd_pSzYpdo6OcT0pyLehCTRriQeyK6ISkrn3F5wSPy7q5C1_b1W0BJgnrfhUegalAErZj7WrmiW_r-Qv5l3UunA0SsrFSLsOV644rY5Q8-46HtD8hR). In 1699, hundreds of French refugees established Manakin Towne on 10,000 acres of land along the James River, which had previously been inhabited by the Monacan Native American tribe[11](https://griffithpreservation.com/manakin-towne-french-settlement-on-the-james/), [12](https://www.newrivernotes.com/hugenot-emigration-to-virginia-and-settlement-at-manakin-town/). Individual Huguenots also settled in Virginia before and after 1700[12](https://www.newrivernotes.com/hugenot-emigration-to-virginia-and-settlement-at-manakin-town/). Some landowners and speculators, such as William Byrd I and Daniel Coxe, sought to recruit Huguenots to settle on their lands[13](http://s1030794421.onlinehome.us/religion/huguenots.html), [12](https://www.newrivernotes.com/hugenot-emigration-to-virginia-and-settlement-at-manakin-town/). There were also offers from individuals to sell large tracts of land to French immigrants, even providing financial assistance for building houses and initial sustenance[13](http://s1030794421.onlinehome.us/religion/huguenots.html). Action Step 2.command: execution = tool.execute(query="Who did the Huguenots in the state primary Edwards won outside of Georgia buy land from?")
result:
In the state of South Carolina, where John Edwards won the Democratic primary in 2004[1](https://www.wral.com/story/1090358/), [2](https://www.sfgate.com/politics/article/Strong-showing-puts-Edwards-in-2nd-place-Big-2802150.php), [3](https://journals.shareok.org/arp/ojs/arp/article/download/382/359), [4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Edwards_2004_presidential_campaign), Huguenots primarily acquired land from the Lords Proprietors of Carolina and other individual landowners.

Huguenots were drawn to South Carolina by the promise of affordable land, commercial prospects, and religious freedom[5](https://columbiametro.com/article/defining-carolina/), [6](https://www.scencyclopedia.org/sce/entries/huguenots/). The Lords Proprietors actively encouraged Huguenot immigration, granting them land upon their arrival[7](https://vertexaisearch.cloud.google.com/grounding-api-redirect/AUZIYQEtymBO05EpMwHsWSwD3RGq10dvQWxFP93Rsx0WMdgtFxaOafHOJh7-jMafblSYK-lSOp2-R2ygto9oW6SqkUx0MBi58zA1zR9ydkJ0-SK4WpxnBqhGTKqt3jfGXSwdvyWNVGGTgTw_cfT-uEF_7L-3lrMHPFv7nxBKFXerHMhcD8RWbuzi), [8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf), [9](https://huguenotsociety.org/blog/passengers-on-the-margaret-1685nbsp-b5lcj), [5](https://columbiametro.com/article/defining-carolina/), [6](https://www.scencyclopedia.org/sce/entries/huguenots/). Many Huguenots purchased land directly from the proprietors in London or from their agents in Carolina[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf).

Examples of land transactions include:
*   King Charles II arranged for Huguenots to be sent to South Carolina in 1679, and some "purchased land from the Proprietors of Carolina"[7](https://vertexaisearch.cloud.google.com/grounding-api-redirect/AUZIYQEtymBO05EpMwHsWSwD3RGq10dvQWxFP93Rsx0WMdgtFxaOafHOJh7-jMafblSYK-lSOp2-R2ygto9oW6SqkUx0MBi58zA1zR9ydkJ0-SK4WpxnBqhGTKqt3jfGXSwdvyWNVGGTgTw_cfT-uEF_7L-3lrMHPFv7nxBKFXerHMhcD8RWbuzi).
*   In 1685, Jean Francis Gignilliat received a grant of 3,000 acres from the proprietors[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). Similarly, Arnold Bruneau was awarded a 3,000-acre land grant by the proprietors in 1686[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf).
*   Individual Huguenots also bought land from others. For instance, Robert Stevens and Bartholomew le Roux purchased 350 acres in September 1683[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). James Le Bas bought 3,000 acres for 150 British pounds in September 1685[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf).
*   Pierre Galliard and his wife bought land in Jamestown, a French and Swiss settlement on the Santee River[10](https://chipstone.org/article.php/289/American-Furniture-1997/Religion,-Artisanry,-and-Cultural-Identity:-The-Huguenot-Experience-in-South-Carolina,-1680%E2%80%931725).
*   John Gaillard acquired a total of 3,780 acres over fourteen years through both grants and purchases, including tracts from his neighbors, in the Santee community[8](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf).
*   Gideon Faucheraud received a land grant in Berkeley County and also purchased land from Susan Dasseex[11](https://sites.rootsweb.com/~scbchs/goose1.html).
*   Jean Carrière, an indentured servant upon arrival, eventually bought or sold over 1,000 acres in Craven and Berkeley Counties.[9](https://huguenotsociety.org/blog/passengers-on-the-margaret-1685nbsp-b5lcj)
*   René Petit and Jacob Guérard were each granted a "Mannour of 4000 Akers" by King James.[9](https://huguenotsociety.org/blog/passengers-on-the-margaret-1685nbsp-b5lcj)

4.
Action Step 3.command: execution = tool.execute(query="Who did the Huguenots in the state primary Edwards won outside of Georgia buy land from?")
result:
The Huguenots, particularly in the state of South Carolina where John Edwards won a primary outside of Georgia in 2004[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Edwards_2004_presidential_campaign), [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Edwards), [3](https://www.newsweek.com/out-steam-123933), [4](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2004/3/3/edwards-quits-us-presidential-race), [5](https://www.theguardian.com/world/2004/feb/04/uselections2004.usa4), acquired land from various sources.

In South Carolina, Huguenots purchased land from individuals and received grants. For example, Pierre Galliard bought land in Jamestown, a French and Swiss settlement on the Santee River[6](https://chipstone.org/article.php/289/American-Furniture-1997/Religion,-Artisanry,-and-Cultural-Identity:-The-Huguenot-Experience-in-South-Carolina,-1680%E2%80%931725). Joachim Gaillard received 600 acres in "James Town precinct" in 1687, and his son, John Gaillard, later acquired 3,780 acres through both grants and purchases[7](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA221160.pdf). Additionally, prominent Huguenot Paul De St. Julien built Hanover House on a 1,000-acre property that was granted by the Lords Proprietors in England between 1714 and 1716[8](https://www.clemson.edu/about/history/properties/documents/hanover-activity.pdf). The Lords Proprietors encouraged Huguenot immigration to Carolina with promises of land and religious toleration, and the main areas of Huguenot settlement included the Santee River, Goose Creek, the Cooper River branches, and Charleston[6](https://chipstone.org/article.php/289/American-Furniture-1997/Religion,-Artisanry,-and-Cultural-Identity:-The-Huguenot-Experience-in-South-Carolina,-1680%E2%80%931725), [9](https://columbiametro.com/article/defining-carolina/).

In Virginia, another state with significant Huguenot settlement, King William sent a large group of Huguenots in 1690, allotting them lands along the James River[10](https://vertexaisearch.cloud.google.com/grounding-api-redirect/AUZIYQGLA_nm9roJkFBSFf06yDoBUVvwcAS090Uzd2zzSfmy-p9CaZjmDbauNSTC2NiYv8xd_pSzYpdo6OcT0pyLehCTRriQeyK6ISkrn3F5wSPy7q5C1_b1W0BJgnrfhUegalAErZj7WrmiW_r-Qv5l3UunA0SsrFSLsOV644rY5Q8-46HtD8hR). In 1699, hundreds of French refugees established Manakin Towne on 10,000 acres of land along the James River, which had previously been inhabited by the Monacan Native American tribe[11](https://griffithpreservation.com/manakin-towne-french-settlement-on-the-james/), [12](https://www.newrivernotes.com/hugenot-emigration-to-virginia-and-settlement-at-manakin-town/). Individual Huguenots also settled in Virginia before and after 1700[12](https://www.newrivernotes.com/hugenot-emigration-to-virginia-and-settlement-at-manakin-town/). Some landowners and speculators, such as William Byrd I and Daniel Coxe, sought to recruit Huguenots to settle on their lands[13](http://s1030794421.onlinehome.us/religion/huguenots.html), [12](https://www.newrivernotes.com/hugenot-emigration-to-virginia-and-settlement-at-manakin-town/). There were also offers from individuals to sell large tracts of land to French immigrants, even providing financial assistance for building houses and initial sustenance[13](http://s1030794421.onlinehome.us/religion/huguenots.html). Sir Edwin Sandys, a Virginia Company founder, encouraged Huguenot settlement and patented 650 acres on the lower James River[14](https://stlukesmuseum.org/edu-blog/huguenots-in-early-virginia/).

### 1. Process Summary
The query was addressed through a multi-step verification process using historical and web search tools:
*   **Initial Analysis & Tool Selection:** The query identified a specific historical event involving the Huguenots, a state primary school named "Edwards" located outside Georgia, and a land purchase. The `Generalist_Solution_Generator_Tool` was used to outline the search path.
*   **Primary Source Retrieval:** The `Wikipedia_RAG_Search_Tool` was executed to retrieve specific details about the "Edwards" school and the Huguenot community. However, the initial results were limited to general historical overviews and did not contain the specific name of the landowner.
*   **Verification and Refinement:** The `Ground_Google_Search_Tool` was used to cross-reference the findings. This tool provided the critical detail that John Edwards won the Democratic primary in South Carolina (outside Georgia) in 2004. It confirmed that historically, Huguenots in South Carolina primarily purchased land from the **Lords Proprietors of Carolina** (and other individual landowners).
*   **Final Synthesis:** By combining the confirmation of the location (South Carolina) with the historical context of land ownership, the specific entity identified as the primary source of the land for the Huguenot community in this region was the Lords Proprietors of Carolina.

### 2. Answer
The Huguenots in the state primary Edwards won outside of Georgia (John Edwards in South Carolina in 2004) primarily bought land from the **Lords Proprietors of Carolina**.
